item.title: Three cheers for scuttling the Jones Act
item.description: The 100-year-old protectionist law deserves more than a temporary waiver.

item.title: Who was behind Iranian girls' school bombing?
item.description: An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.

item.title: Victory?
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on commander in chief Trump, napalm and oil tankers.

item.title: A euphemism for the ages in Virginia
item.description: The bipartisan redistricting rush of 2026 is ennobling neither party.

item.title: Washington state’s tax folly
item.description: Democrats have voted to bring in a state income levy for residents earning over $1 million a year.

item.title: Universities should let students speak
item.description: Plus, readers on investing in infrastructure and war-proofing your budget.

item.title: Why are ultra-processed foods so bad? Answering your questions.
item.description: A dietitian explains why it’s best to limit ultra-processed food.

item.title: Iran’s Islamic Republic 2.0 is coming — and it won’t be pretty
item.description: How Trump’s tactical victory could turn into a forever war.

item.title: In defense of the secret ballot
item.description: One way to clean up the Biden-Big Labor mess.

item.title: The studio renaissance driving this year’s Oscars may already be ending
item.description: How Warner Bros. made “Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” — and what happens next.

item.title: Bill Barr: Federal judges rely on this guide. It just took a sharp left turn.
item.description: The judiciary needs unbiased scientific evidence, not climate advocacy.

item.title: The myth of absolute parental rights
item.description: Age limits inspired by Australia law won’t hamper role adults play in raising children.

item.title: Colleges can’t shirk this essential obligation
item.description: If colleges want taxpayer money, they need to be good stewards of it.

item.title: Pittsburgh is pivoting. It could be a lesson for Democrats.
item.description: The new mayor is putting pragmatism over ideology as the city prepares to host the NFL draft.

item.title: How Ro Khanna turned a sex trafficking scandal into a campaign stunt
item.description: The California Democrat’s ambitions find fuel in the flames of conspiracy.

item.title: Vietnam’s Communists remember that socialism doesn’t work
item.description: Hanoi’s rulers are looking to the private sector to drive growth.

item.title: Many wealthy Americans already pay European tax rates
item.description: The top marginal rates in Manhattan and San Francisco are higher than in Amsterdam, Berlin or Milan.

item.title: Gavin Newsom has a hold-a-wolf-by-the-ears problem
item.description: If the California governor runs for president, he’ll face a tricky test coping with progressives.

item.title: Who decides when we go to war? Here’s what the framers said.
item.description: The Constitution on war powers, executive power, DHS funding and Kennedy style, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: How elevator rules are throwing a wrench into America’s housing market
item.description: The unintended consequences of a 1988 law are making housing less accessible and driving up prices.

item.title: Innovation is the way out of China’s chokehold on critical minerals
item.description: Leaning into one of America’s biggest strengths is the best way to wean off a dangerous dependency.

item.title: The hidden reason Republicans could outperform midterm expectations
item.description: Why analysts keep getting Trump’s approval numbers wrong.

item.title: Our Air Force is stretched dangerously thin. Here’s how to revamp it.
item.description: Chronic underinvestment has left the Air Force perilously thin in aircraft, munitions and crews.

item.title: You bet this is a war of choice. Just not America’s.
item.description: U.S. military action in Iran is justified because of longstanding armed conflict.

item.title: Even now, America can’t rally. What has Trump done?
item.description: The country is at war. It’s the 250th. America is still in no mood.

item.title: What is the definition of victory in Iran? There are three.
item.description: A patient Trump can disarm the regime, topple it — and hand the country back to its people.

item.title: Is flying today worse than in the 1950s? Define ‘worse.’
item.description: Coach comfort has declined, but price and access are better.

item.title: How America keeps reinventing itself
item.description: How can the U.S. lead in rebuilding industrial capacity? Christian Keil believes the answer lies with American dynamism. He is a partner at a16z, who sees technology as a key to our future. Even through uncertain times and sharp competition from China. He joins host Megan McArdle to discuss his experience — from innovating satellite technology during his time at Astranis to now investing in companies through venture capital. He also gives more detail on his recently published the report "More Perfect," which explores how technology has shaped and will reshape America.

item.title: Don’t expect cheaper concert tickets
item.description: Regulators trying to solve a “problem” have created new ones.
